result:
I keep coming back to the idea that every sentence is a small act of resurrection. When I type “the rusted gate creaked open,” something that never happened—something that never even existed—suddenly has a body made of sound and iron and dusk light. It’s an ordinary miracle, but it still makes me pause, fingers hovering over the keys like a conjurer afraid of the next trick.

There’s a warehouse down by the river that isn’t on any map. I pass it sometimes when I’m jogging off the grief of a bad week. Its windows are painted black from the inside, and a single letter—“K”—has been sloppily whitewashed on the rolling door. I imagine the interior is a single, impossible room: a prairie under a different century’s sky, or a coral reef humming thirty feet above the concrete floor. I never try the handle; the mystery is a lantern I’d rather carry than extinguish.

Tonight the city smells of wet asphalt and roasted coffee, as if someone brewed an entire district by accident. Sirens braid with the laughter leaking from bar doorways; the sound is a rope thrown back and forth between despair and delight. I think of how we’re all porous to these moments—how a stranger’s whoop of joy can slip through the walls of the self and nudge the heart like a cat insisting on dinner. We pretend we’re separate plots of land, but we’re really one watershed, every emotion draining into the same hidden river.

I keep a notebook of unfinished things: lullabies without last verses, obituaries for people still alive, a recipe for the perfect apology (ingredients include moon-cooled tea and the willingness to admit you were not the protagonist in someone else’s pain). Sometimes I flip through the pages and feel like an archaeologist who can’t decide whether the shards are priceless or landfill. Yet each fragment glows faintly, a firefly that forgot how to finish its own light.

Maybe that’s enough. Maybe the point isn’t to finish but to keep striking the flint of attention against the dark. Somewhere in the warehouse with the impossible prairie, a gate I never described is already rusting into music.
